d_ko: Persistently elevated arterial blood pressure (≥130/80 mmHg); the most commonly managed chronic condition in primary care.
Chronic metabolic disorder of impaired insulin production or action, resulting in hyperglycemia; type 2 is predominant in family practice.
Proactive health services (vaccinations, screenings, counseling) aimed at preventing disease before it develops.
The simultaneous presence of two or more chronic conditions in a single patient.
Ongoing, coordinated care strategies to control long-lasting conditions and maintain quality of life.
Excess body fat accumulation (BMI ≥30) associated with increased risk of diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and joint problems.
Acute infection of the nose, throat, and airways; the single most frequent reason for primary care visits worldwide.
Patient's voluntary agreement to a proposed medical intervention after receiving adequate information.
The primary symptom or concern that brings a patient to seek medical care, recorded at the start of every encounter.
A comprehensive record of a patient's past illnesses, surgeries, medications, allergies, and family health background.
The clinical process of weighing probabilities of similar conditions to isolate the most likely diagnosis from others sharing similar signs and symptoms.
A forecast or prediction of the probable course and eventual outcome of a disease, including likelihood of recovery or progression.
Hypersensitivity immune response to a substance (allergen) that is typically harmless to most people.
A written or electronic instruction from a licensed clinician specifying a medication, dose, route, and duration.
Subjective evidence of disease experienced and reported by the patient (e.g. pain, fatigue, nausea).
Objective evidence of disease detected by a clinician during examination (e.g. fever, swelling, abnormal heart sound).
Core clinical measurements: blood pressure, heart rate, respiratory rate, temperature, and oxygen saturation.
Condition of rapid onset, short duration, and often severe symptoms — contrasted with chronic.
Gross necrosis of myocardial tissue resulting from the abrupt cessation of coronary blood flow, causing chest pain, cardiac dysfunction, and potentially fatal arrhythmias.
Umbrella term for conditions caused by sudden reduced blood flow to the heart, including unstable angina and MI.
Paroxysmal chest pain or discomfort caused by transient myocardial ischemia, typically triggered by exertion or stress.

Syndrome in which the heart cannot pump sufficient blood to meet the body's metabolic demands, leading to fluid accumulation, breathlessness, and fatigue; classified as systolic (reduced ejection fraction) or diastolic (preserved ejection fraction).
Any abnormality in the rate or rhythm of the heartbeat, ranging from benign to life-threatening.
A common supraventricular tachyarrhythmia characterized by rapid, uncoordinated, and chaotic electrical activation of the cardiac atria, causing irregular heartbeat and stroke risk.
Diagnostic test recording electrical activity of the heart; essential for detecting arrhythmias, MI, and conduction abnormalities.
Narrowing or blockage of the coronary arteries due to atherosclerosis, leading to reduced blood supply to the heart.
Build-up of plaques (fat, cholesterol, calcium) inside arterial walls, narrowing vessels and increasing risk of MI and stroke.
Sudden loss of neurological function due to an interruption of blood supply to the brain; classified as ischemic or hemorrhagic.
Ischemic stroke caused by blockage of a cerebral artery; accounts for ~85% of all strokes.

Stroke caused by rupture of a blood vessel within or around the brain, causing bleeding into brain tissue (intracerebral) or the subarachnoid space; accounts for ~15% of all strokes with high mortality.
Insufficient blood supply to a tissue or organ, causing oxygen deprivation and risk of cell death.
Brief episode of neurological dysfunction from temporary loss of blood flow to the brain, resolving within 24 hours; a major warning sign for stroke.
Sudden cessation of effective heart pumping, causing loss of consciousness and death within minutes without CPR.
Escape of blood from ruptured blood vessels, which may occur internally or externally; classified by source (arterial, venous, capillary) and location.
Subjective awareness of an abnormally fast, slow, or irregular heartbeat; often a presenting symptom of arrhythmia or anxiety.
The process of identifying a disease or condition based on symptoms, signs, history, and test results.
Any intervention aimed at curing, managing, or alleviating a disease or its symptoms.
Describing a condition that persists over a long period (generally three months or more) and typically requires ongoing monitoring and management.
The body's immune response to injury or infection, characterised by redness, swelling, heat, and pain.
Invasion and multiplication of pathogenic microorganisms (bacteria, viruses, fungi) in the body.
Body temperature above 38°C (100.4°F), typically indicating infection or systemic inflammation.
Unpleasant sensory and emotional experience associated with actual or potential tissue damage.
The surgical removal and microscopic examination of a small tissue sample from a living body for diagnostic purposes, especially to detect cancer.
Describing a tumour or condition that is non-cancerous, does not invade nearby tissues, and does not spread to other parts of the body; generally associated with a favourable outcome.
Describing a tumour that invades surrounding tissue and can metastasize to other parts of the body.
Spread of cancer cells from the primary site to distant organs or tissues via blood or lymphatic system.
A break or crack in a bone caused by trauma, stress, or underlying bone disease such as osteoporosis.
Abnormal accumulation of fluid in body tissues causing visible swelling, commonly in the legs or lungs.
Unpleasant sensation in the stomach with an urge to vomit; a common symptom across many conditions.
Subjective sensation of difficulty or discomfort in breathing; a cardinal symptom of cardiac and respiratory disease.
Process of rapidly assessing and prioritising patients based on severity of condition to allocate care efficiently.
Progressive decline in cognitive function severe enough to interfere with daily life; Alzheimer's is the most common cause.
Most common form of dementia, characterized by amyloid plaque accumulation and progressive memory and cognitive decline.
Chronic neurological disorder characterized by recurrent, unprovoked seizures due to abnormal electrical brain activity.
A sudden, uncontrolled electrical disturbance in the brain causing changes in behaviour, movement, sensation, or consciousness; a symptom rather than a diagnosis in itself.
Progressive neurodegenerative disorder causing tremor, rigidity, and bradykinesia due to loss of dopamine-producing neurons.
Autoimmune disease in which the immune system attacks the myelin sheath of nerve fibers, disrupting brain-body communication.
Pain in any region of the head; classified as primary (migraine, tension) or secondary (due to underlying condition).
Recurrent moderate-to-severe headache, often unilateral and throbbing, frequently accompanied by nausea and light sensitivity.
Damage or dysfunction of peripheral nerves causing numbness, tingling, weakness, or pain, commonly due to diabetes.
Decline in memory, language, judgment, or other thinking skills, ranging from mild (MCI) to severe (dementia).
Broad specialty focused on the prevention, diagnosis, and treatment of adult diseases not requiring surgery.
Medical specialty dealing with diagnosis and treatment of mental, emotional, and behavioural disorders.
Specialty devoted to the health and medical care of infants, children, and adolescents up to age 18.
Combined specialty covering pregnancy and childbirth (obstetrics) and female reproductive health (gynecology).
Specialty that uses operative techniques to treat injuries, diseases, and deformities of the body.
Specialty concerned with conditions of the musculoskeletal system including bones, joints, ligaments, tendons, and muscles.
Specialty dealing with disorders of the nervous system, including the brain, spinal cord, and peripheral nerves.
Medical specialty focused on diseases and conditions of the heart and cardiovascular system, including diagnosis, treatment, and prevention of cardiac disorders.
Specialty dedicated to the study, diagnosis, and treatment of cancer.
Abnormal levels of lipids (cholesterol, triglycerides) in the blood, a key cardiovascular risk factor.
Formal process of directing a patient from a primary care provider to a specialist for further evaluation or treatment.
The concurrent use of multiple medications by a single patient, increasing interaction risks and adverse effects; commonly defined as five or more drugs simultaneously.
Administration of a vaccine to stimulate immunity and protect against specific infectious diseases.
A deficiency in circulating thyroid hormones leading to a generalized metabolic slowdown, causing fatigue, weight gain, and cold intolerance.
A systemic skeletal disorder characterized by low bone mass and bone fragility, increasing susceptibility to fractures especially in postmenopausal women and older adults.
A mood disorder characterised by persistent low mood, loss of interest or pleasure, and functional impairment lasting at least two weeks; one of the leading causes of disability worldwide.
Bacterial infection of the urinary system (bladder, urethra, kidneys); one of the most common infectious diagnoses.
Weight (kg) divided by height squared (m²), used as a screening tool for underweight, overweight, and obesity.
Medical care provided without hospital admission; the patient visits a clinic and returns home the same day.
Scheduled subsequent visit to monitor a patient's condition, treatment response, or recovery progress.
Testing of asymptomatic individuals to detect disease or risk factors early, before symptoms develop.
The degree to which a patient correctly follows the prescribed treatment plan, including medications and lifestyle changes.
Persistent difficulty initiating or maintaining sleep, causing daytime impairment.
Pain localised to the lumbar region, classified as acute (under 6 weeks), subacute, or chronic (over 12 weeks); one of the leading causes of disability and primary care visits globally.
Group of mental disorders characterised by excessive fear or worry that interferes with daily functioning.
A chronic digestive disease where stomach acid or bile flows back into the food pipe, causing heartburn and potential mucosal damage.
A reduction in the number of erythrocytes, concentration of haemoglobin, or haematocrit, resulting in fatigue, pallor, and reduced oxygen delivery to tissues.
A non-invasive diagnostic procedure utilizing ultrasound waves to evaluate the real-time structure and function of the heart.
Minimally invasive procedure to open blocked coronary arteries, typically using a balloon and stent.
Percentage of blood pumped out of the left ventricle with each heartbeat; key measure of heart function (normal ≥55%).
Medication that reduces the blood's ability to clot, used to prevent stroke in AFib and treat deep vein thrombosis.
Implantable electronic device that delivers electrical impulses to regulate abnormally slow or irregular heartbeats.
Chronic syndrome where the cardiac output falls short of physiological metabolic demands, accumulating back-pressure fluid in lungs and limbs.
Emergency procedure combining chest compressions and rescue breaths to maintain circulation and oxygenation during cardiac arrest.
Imaging technique using strong magnetic fields and radio waves to produce detailed cross-sectional images of organs, soft tissues, bones, and the nervous system.

Imaging technique using X-rays to produce detailed cross-sectional images of internal structures; widely used for brain, chest, abdomen, and bone assessment, and first-line in acute trauma and stroke.
A diagnostic procedure to collect cerebrospinal fluid by inserting a needle between lumbar vertebrae; used for meningitis, subarachnoid hemorrhage diagnosis.
Acute inflammation of the protective membranes covering the brain and spinal cord, usually caused by bacterial or viral infection.
An illusory sensation of spinning of oneself or the environment due to vestibular pathology; distinct from non-specific dizziness.
Non-specific sensation of lightheadedness or unsteadiness without a true spinning quality; distinct from vertigo.
Group of diseases characterized by progressive loss of nerve cell structure or function, including Alzheimer's and Parkinson's.
Damage to the spinal cord resulting in loss of motor, sensory, or autonomic function below the level of injury.
Recording of electrical activity of the brain via scalp electrodes; primary diagnostic tool for epilepsy and seizure disorders.
Impairment of language ability caused by brain damage, affecting speaking, understanding, reading, or writing.
Involuntary rhythmic muscle contraction causing shaking of one or more body parts; a hallmark symptom of Parkinson's disease.
A specific situation or pre-existing clinical condition in which a drug, procedure, or intervention must not be used due to risk of harm.
A drug that relieves pain without causing loss of consciousness; includes NSAIDs, acetaminophen, and opioids.
A medication that reduces elevated body temperature (fever); commonly combined with analgesics (e.g. acetaminophen, ibuprofen).
Route of drug or fluid administration directly into a vein, allowing rapid onset; used for urgent treatments and hydration.
An unintended, harmful response occurring at normal therapeutic doses of a drug; distinct from overdose or medication error.
Action taken or medication administered to prevent the occurrence or development of disease before it manifests.
Drug administration into the fatty tissue just beneath the skin; slower absorption than IV; used for insulin, heparin.
Drug administration directly into muscle tissue, providing faster absorption than subcutaneous; used for vaccines and some antibiotics.
Drug delivery by placing medication under the tongue for rapid absorption through the oral mucosa into the bloodstream, bypassing first-pass metabolism.
The ability of a drug or treatment to produce the desired therapeutic effect under controlled conditions; distinguished from effectiveness, which refers to real-world performance.
Subspecialty dealing with disorders of the endocrine glands and hormones, including diabetes and thyroid disease.
Specialty focused on conditions affecting the skin, hair, nails, and mucous membranes.
Specialty dealing with the anatomy, physiology, and diseases of the eye.
Specialty dealing with diseases of the ear, nose, throat, and related structures of the head and neck.
Specialty focused on the urinary tract in both sexes and the male reproductive system.
Specialty using medical imaging (X-ray, CT, MRI, ultrasound) to diagnose and guide treatment of disease.
Specialty focused on the immediate diagnosis and treatment of acute illnesses and injuries that require urgent care.
Specialty responsible for administering anaesthesia and managing pain before, during, and after surgical procedures.
Specialty that examines tissues, organs, and bodily fluids to diagnose disease, often via biopsy or autopsy.
Study of how drugs interact with biological systems; foundational to prescribing and understanding medication effects.
Subspecialty focused on the diagnosis and treatment of infections caused by bacteria, viruses, fungi, and parasites.
Healthcare profession focused on the diagnosis, prevention, and treatment of diseases of the teeth, gums, and mouth.
Specialty aimed at restoring function and quality of life in patients with physical impairments due to injury, illness, or disability.
Class of medications that block adrenaline receptors to slow heart rate and reduce blood pressure.
Blood tests (e.g. troponin, CK-MB) measuring proteins released by damaged heart muscle, used to diagnose MI.
A patient's ability to obtain, process, and understand basic health information to make appropriate health decisions.
Legal document stating a patient's wishes regarding medical treatment in the event they become unable to communicate.
Long-term, consistent relationship between a patient and their primary care provider.
Specialised care focused on relieving symptoms and improving quality of life for patients with serious illness, regardless of prognosis.
Surgical specialty focused on reconstructing or altering the human body for aesthetic or functional restoration purposes.
Surgical specialty dealing with organs in the chest including the lungs, heart, oesophagus, and great vessels.
Surgical specialty for treating conditions of the brain, spinal cord, and peripheral nervous system.
Subspecialty focused on autoimmune and inflammatory diseases of the joints, muscles, and connective tissue.
Subspecialty focused on diseases of the respiratory tract and lungs.
Subspecialty focused on kidney diseases, electrolyte disorders, hypertension, and renal replacement therapy.
Subspecialty focused on the digestive system including the stomach, intestines, liver, pancreas, and gallbladder.
Subspecialty dealing with diseases of the blood and blood-forming organs, including anaemia, clotting disorders, and leukaemia.
Specialty using radioactive substances to visualise and treat diseases, including PET and bone scans.
A temporary loss of consciousness and muscle tone caused by a transient drop in blood flow to the brain.
Sharp, stabbing chest pain that worsens with deep inspiration, coughing, or moving the thorax; indicates pleural inflammation.
The vomiting of bright red blood or "coffee-ground" material, indicating acute bleeding in the upper gastrointestinal tract.
The coughing up of blood or blood-stained sputum originating from the bronchi, larynx, trachea, or lungs.
The passage of dark, black, tarry stools containing altered blood, indicative of upper gastrointestinal bleeding.
The passage of fresh, bright red blood per rectum, usually indicating acute lower gastrointestinal bleeding.
An unpleasant, irritating sensation of the skin that provokes the urge to scratch; may be localised or generalised.
A bluish discoloration of the skin and mucous membranes resulting from inadequate oxygenation of the blood.
A hollow, muscular tube connecting the oral and nasal cavities to the esophagus and larynx, serving as a common pathway for food and air.
The cartilaginous organ located at the top of the trachea that houses the vocal cords and controls airflow during breathing and phonation.
A large membranous tube reinforced by rings of cartilage, extending from the larynx to the bronchi, conducting air to and from the lungs.
The muscular tube that propels swallowed food and liquids from the pharynx down to the stomach via peristalsis.
The first part of the small intestine immediately beyond the stomach, where chyme mixes with bile and pancreatic enzymes.
The highly vascularized middle segment of the small intestine specialized primarily for nutrient absorption.
The final segment of the small intestine, primarily responsible for absorbing vitamin B12 and bile salts; connects to the large intestine at the ileocecal valve.
The major endocrine master gland at the base of the brain controlling development and downstream hormone secretion from other glands throughout the body.
A primary lymphoid organ in the superior mediastinum where bone marrow-derived T-lymphocytes differentiate and mature.
A small, pear-shaped organ underneath the liver that stores and concentrates bile before releasing it into the duodenum during digestion.
A non-cancerous enlargement of the prostate gland common in aging men, leading to lower urinary tract symptoms such as weak stream and frequency.
The abnormal presence of air in the pleural cavity causing lung collapse; may be spontaneous, traumatic, or iatrogenic.
The presence or formation of calculi within the gallbladder cavity, which may cause biliary colic or progress to cholecystitis.
A progressive, poorly reversible lung disease characterized by chronic airflow limitation, typically driven by smoking.
A chronic systemic autoimmune disorder characterized by symmetric inflammatory arthritis, primarily targeting synovial joints.
Visual examination of interior structures or hollow organs of the body using a flexible optical instrument (endoscope).
A minimally invasive surgical technique performed in the abdomen or pelvis through small incisions utilizing a camera.
The placement of a flexible plastic tube into the trachea past the vocal cords to maintain an open airway and assist ventilation.
A clinical procedure where a needle or catheter is inserted into the peritoneal cavity to sample or drain ascitic fluid.
The clinical act of listening to the internal sounds of the body, usually using a stethoscope, to assess circulatory or respiratory status.
The physical examination process of utilizing pressure from the hands and fingers to feel organs, masses, or tender areas.
A glycoprotein manufactured by the prostate gland; blood concentrations serve as a clinical marker for BPH or malignancy screening.
Acute or chronic inflammation of the gallbladder wall, typically secondary to cystic duct obstruction by gallstones.
Acute inflammation of the vermiform appendix, presenting with lower right quadrant abdominal pain, nausea, and fever; managed surgically or, in uncomplicated cases, with antibiotics.
Inflammation of the pancreas, characterized by premature activation of pancreatic enzymes leading to autodigestion of the organ tissue.
Inflammatory process affecting the peritoneum, most commonly due to bacterial infection following perforation of an abdominal organ.
The formation of crystalline calculi within the renal pelvis or calyces, causing severe flank pain, hematuria, and urinary obstruction.
Gradual, progressive, and irreversible loss of renal function lasting for three months or longer.
Total, permanent failure of the kidneys, requiring long-term renal replacement therapy (dialysis or kidney transplantation).
A medical procedure that uses a dialyzer machine to filter toxins, waste products, and excess fluids from the patient's blood when the kidneys fail.
A method of removing waste and excess fluid from the blood using the patient's peritoneum as a semipermeable membrane across which fluids exchange.
Distension and dilation of the renal pelvis caused by obstruction of urine outflow, leading to potential kidney damage if untreated.
A disorder of the inner ear characterized by brief, recurrent episodes of spinning vertigo triggered by specific changes in head position.
An inflammation or bacterial/viral infection of the middle ear space, frequently presenting with fluid accumulation behind the tympanic membrane.
The perception of noise or ringing in the ears or head when no external sound is present; may be continuous or intermittent.
Bleeding originating from the interior of the nasal cavity, most commonly from the Kiesselbach plexus on the anterior nasal septum.
Inflammation of the mucosal lining of the paranasal sinuses, resulting from infection, allergy, or autoimmune issues.
A visual disorder where the eyes point in different directions and fail to remain aligned simultaneously under normal viewing conditions.
Progressive opacification or clouding of the natural crystalline lens of the eye, obstructing the transmission of light onto the retina.
A group of eye diseases characterized by progressive optic nerve damage and visual field loss, often associated with elevated intraocular pressure.
An involuntary, rapid, and rhythmic oscillation of the eyeballs, which may be horizontal, vertical, or rotatory.
Progressive deterioration of the macula (the central area of the retina), causing loss of central vision while peripheral vision remains intact.
Redness of the skin or mucous membranes caused by increased blood flow in superficial capillaries, associated with inflammation or infection.
A vascular reaction of the skin characterized by the transient appearance of elevated, pruritic wheals caused by histamine release.
An acute, spreading bacterial infection of the deep dermis and subcutaneous tissue, presenting with redness, warmth, swelling, and pain.
A chronic, pruritic, inflammatory skin disease that typically begins in infancy or childhood and follows a relapsing course.
A chronic autoimmune skin condition accelerating skin cell production, resulting in thick, silvery scales and itchy, dry plaques.
An abnormal dermal sensation (such as tingling, pricking, or numbness) experienced without any clear external sensory stimulus.
Severe radiating pain originating in the lower back and traveling down the path of the sciatic nerve through the buttock and posterior leg.
Form of inflammatory arthritis caused by the deposition of monosodium urate crystals in joints and tissues, often primary in the first metatarsophalangeal joint.
An abnormal excessive outward curvature of the thoracic region of the spine, leading to a slouching posture or hunchback.
A clinical condition caused by sustained overproduction of thyroid hormones, leading to a hypermetabolic state with symptoms including weight loss, palpitations, heat intolerance, and tremor.
A metabolic disorder caused by prolonged systemic exposure to abnormally elevated levels of glucocorticoids (cortisol).
A rare disorder of water metabolism caused by a deficiency of vasopressin (ADH) secretion or renal resistance to it, causing polyuria and polydipsia.
Progressive microvascular complications of long-standing diabetes mellitus affecting the blood vessels of the retina.
An abnormal reduction in the number of platelets in peripheral blood, increasing bleeding risk and causing petechiae or easy bruising.
An increase in the number of circulating white blood cells above normal range, typically a response to infection or severe inflammation.
A chronic bone marrow disorder causing uncontrolled overproduction of red blood cells, increasing blood viscosity and risk of thrombosis and stroke.
Formation of a blood clot within a deep vein, most commonly occurring within the deep tissues of the calves or thighs.
The sudden blockage of a pulmonary artery branch by a blood clot, usually originating from deep vein thrombosis, causing acute respiratory distress.
A catastrophic, life-threatening vascular condition where the inner layer of the aorta tears, creating a false blood-filled channel within the arterial wall.
The surgical excision and extraction of the gallbladder, typically to treat symptomatic cholelithiasis or cholecystitis.
The emergency or elective surgical removal of the vermiform appendix to cure acute appendicitis.
An invasive extraction procedure where a needle or catheter is inserted into the pleural space to drain pathologic accumulations of pleural fluid.
The surgical extraction and removal of all or part of the uterus, commonly indicated for fibroids, endometriosis, or gynecological malignancies.
A specialized, low-dose X-ray imaging procedure used to visualize internal breast tissue structures for early detection of cancer.
An endoscopic examination evaluating the inner lining of the large intestine and rectum via a flexible video scope.
A fatty deposit or plaque that forms on the inner lining of an arterial wall, characteristic of atherosclerosis.
Abnormal enlargement of the heart, usually resulting from chronic hypertension or heart failure.
A cardiac arrhythmia characterized by a slow heart rate, typically fewer than 60 beats per minute in adults.
A cardiac arrhythmia characterized by a rapid heart rate, typically exceeding 100 beats per minute in adults at rest.
A sudden drop in blood pressure (typically >20 mmHg systolic or >10 mmHg diastolic) that occurs when transitioning to an upright position.
Inflammation of the pericardium, the fibroserous sac surrounding the heart, often presenting with chest pain that improves when leaning forward.
An infection of the endocardium, most commonly involving the heart valves, typically caused by bacteria entering the bloodstream.
Cramping pain and weakness in the legs induced by exercise and relieved by rest, caused by peripheral artery disease.
A localized, abnormal dilation or ballooning of a blood vessel wall, carrying a permanent risk of rupture.
Collapse or incomplete expansion of lung tissue, reducing or eliminating gas exchange in the affected area; common postoperatively and in patients with mucus plugging or compression.
A chronic, progressive respiratory disease characterized by permanent dilation and destruction of the bronchial walls.
An abnormal accumulation of fluid in the pleural cavity surrounding the lungs.
A collection of whole blood in the pleural cavity, usually resulting from trauma or chest surgery.
A collection of purulent fluid (pus) within a naturally existing anatomical cavity, most commonly the pleural space.
Abnormally elevated carbon dioxide (CO2) levels in arterial blood (PaCO2 >45 mmHg), typically indicating respiratory failure or hypoventilation.
Abnormally low oxygen levels in arterial blood (PaO2 <80 mmHg or SpO2 <95%), indicating impaired respiratory or circulatory function.
A harsh, high-pitched, vibrating respiratory sound caused by turbulent airflow through a partially obstructed upper airway.
A chronic, progressive respiratory disease where the alveoli become scarred and thickened, obstructing gas exchange.
An abnormal accumulation of protein-rich fluid within the peritoneal cavity.
Late-stage scarring (fibrosis) of the liver tissue caused by chronic liver disease, compromising structural liver functions.
Sensation of difficulty or impairment in the passage of solid or liquid food from the mouth to the stomach.
Inflammation or infection of small pouches (diverticula) that can form along the walls of the intestines.
A yellowish discoloration of the skin, sclera, and mucous membranes caused by hyperbilirubinemia.
A serious condition in which part of the intestine slides or telescopes into an adjacent part, obstructing blood supply.
A loop of intestine twisting around itself and the mesentery that supports it, resulting in mechanical bowel obstruction.
Any bleeding originating from the digestive tract, classified broadly into upper (UGIB) and lower (LGIB).
A chronic syndrome characterized by delayed gastric emptying of solids or liquids without mechanical obstruction.
An abnormal accumulation of cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) within the ventricles of the brain, increasing intracranial pressure.
Lack of voluntary coordination of muscle movements, frequently resulting from cerebellar dysfunction or nerve damage.
Intense, typically burning or stabbing pain radiating along the course of one or more specific nerves.
A motor speech disorder caused by neurological injury to the motor component of the speech system, causing slurred, slow, or imprecise pronunciation despite intact language comprehension.

A broad term for any condition causing altered brain function or structure, presenting with confusion, cognitive decline, or impaired consciousness; may be caused by metabolic, toxic, infectious, or structural factors.
Acute inflammation of the active brain parenchyma, typically caused by viral infection.
The presence of abnormal amounts of protein in the urine, often an early clinical indicator of renal disease.
The abnormal presence of red blood cells in the urine, categorized into gross (visible) and microscopic.
The production of abnormally small amounts of urine, usually defined as less than 400 or 500 mL per day in adults.
Non-passage of urine, clinically defined as less than 100 mL of urine output per day; indicates severe kidney failure.
Immunologically mediated inflammation of the glomeruli, the microscopic filtering units of the kidney.
A collection of clinical findings caused by kidney damage, including massive proteinuria, hypoalbuminemia, hyperlipidemia, and severe edema.
An inflammation of the kidney substance and pelvis, typically resulting from an ascending bacterial infection originating in the urinary tract.
Inflammation of the urinary bladder wall, most frequently caused by an ascending bacterial infection.
The involuntary leakage or loss of control of urine, heavily subcategorized into stress, urge, and overflow types.
Pain, burning, or discomfort experienced during the process of urination, typical of lower urinary tract infections.
Abnormally elevated blood glucose levels (fasting >126 mg/dL or random >200 mg/dL); a hallmark of diabetes mellitus and a risk factor for acute and chronic complications.
Abnormally low blood glucose levels (typically <70 mg/dL), causing autonomic symptoms (sweating, tremor) and neuroglycopenic symptoms (confusion, loss of consciousness).
A life-threatening, acute metabolic complication of diabetes mellitus characterized by severe hyperglycemia, metabolic acidosis, and high blood ketone levels.
An abnormal benign proliferation of glandular breast tissue in males, caused by an imbalance between estrogen and androgen action.
A chronic disorder caused by excessive production of growth hormone (GH) by a pituitary adenoma after the closure of epiphyseal growth plates.
The spontaneous, inappropriate secretion of breast milk unrelated to childbearing or nursing, often linked to hyperprolactinemia.
Excessive growth of thick, dark hair in females following a male-like distribution pattern, resulting from androgen excess.
Overactivity of the parathyroid glands resulting in excess production of parathyroid hormone (PTH), causing hypercalcemia.
A rare, neuroendocrine tumor of the adrenal medulla or extra-adrenal chromaffin tissue that secretes catecholamines.
A cluster of metabolic risk factors—including abdominal obesity, insulin resistance, dyslipidemia, and hypertension—elevating cardiovascular risks.
A simultaneous reduction in red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets, typically caused by bone marrow failure, nutritional deficiency, or malignancy.
An abnormally low concentration of neutrophils (a type of white blood cell) in the blood, severely raising bacterial infection risks.
Disease or abnormal enlargement of lymph nodes, often reacting to localized infection, inflammation, or malignancy.
Pathological enlargement of the spleen, commonly resulting from portal hypertension, hematologic malignancies, or chronic infections.
A grave pathological systemic process where widespread microvascular blood clotting depletes clotting factors, resulting in severe hemorrhage.
A malignant neoplasm (cancer) that originates from epithelial cells, which line internal organs and cover the body.
A malignant tumor arising from mesenchymal supportive or connective tissues, such as bone, cartilage, fat, or muscle.
A type of cancer treatment that stimulates or restores the body's natural immune system mechanisms to detect and fight malignant cells.
Additional cancer treatment given after the primary treatment (such as surgery) to lower the risk of recurrence.
Treatment, such as chemotherapy or radiation, administered as a first step to shrink a tumor before the main primary surgical intervention.
The abnormal pathological absence of menstruation, classified into primary and secondary types based on whether menarche has occurred.
Painful menstruation or severe uterine cramping during menstrual bleeding, causing functional impairment.
A benign non-cancerous tumor of the uterine smooth muscle tissue, extremely common in women of reproductive age.
The premature separation of a normally implanted placenta from the uterine wall prior to the second stage of labor.
Heavy blood loss exceeding 500 mL (vaginal) or 1000 mL (C-section) within 24 hours following childbirth.
A rapid, severe, and potentially life-threatening systemic hypersensitivity reaction causing airway occlusion and vascular collapse.
Describing a disease or condition that arises spontaneously with no identifiable or known cause.
An adverse condition, illness, or injury induced inadvertently by a physician, medical team, drug therapy, or diagnostic procedure.
An increase in the size of an organ or tissue caused by enlargement of its individual cells rather than an increase in cell number; commonly seen in cardiac muscle in response to increased workload.
An abnormal increase in tissue or organ volume caused by an elevated rate of cellular division, resulting in a higher headcount of normal cells.
A life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection; defined clinically by an acute change in SOFA score ≥2 points.
The inability to process sensory information and recognize objects or persons despite intact sensory organs.
A neurological condition in which a person with a disability or illness is unaware of or denies their own impairment; commonly seen after stroke or in dementia.
A behavioral syndrome characterized by abnormal movements, immobility, mutism, or extreme unresponsiveness.
A movement disorder characterized by a subjective feeling of inner restlessness and a compelling need to move.
A neurological disorder featuring involuntary, repetitive facial movements caused by long-term neuroleptic drug use.
An abnormal, painful, or intolerant sensitivity of the eyes to light.
The gradual, progressive, and bilateral loss of hearing that occurs naturally with aging.
The perception of two images of a single object; commonly caused by extraocular muscle weakness.
A progressive joint disease characterized by degradation of articular cartilage, subchondral bone remodelling, and low-grade inflammation, causing pain and reduced mobility.
A chronic, progressive inflammatory arthritis affecting the sacroiliac joints and axial skeleton.
The degenerative loss of skeletal muscle mass, quality, and strength associated with aging.
Pain, aching, or tenderness in one or more muscles; may be localised (e.g. trauma) or diffuse (e.g. influenza, fibromyalgia, statin use).
A structural lateral curvature of the spine exceeding 10 degrees by Cobb angle, typically presenting during adolescent growth spurts; may be idiopathic, congenital, or neuromuscular in origin.
The cellular death of bone components due to temporary or permanent interruption of blood supply.
Inflammation of a tendon and its protective fluid-filled sheath from repetitive strain.
The partial or complete absence or loss of hair from areas where it normally grows.
Pinpoint, round, non-raised purplish-red spots caused by minor intradermal hemorrhage.
Hemorrhages into the skin producing patches of purplish discoloration that do not blanch under pressure.
A subcutaneous spot of bleeding larger than 1 centimeter in diameter resulting from vascular leakage.
A rapidly progressive, life-threatening destructive infection of subcutaneous tissue and fascia.
A gynecological emergency involving rotation of the ovary, compromising arterial inflow.
A transient loss of consciousness mediated by an inappropriate reflex causing bradycardia and vasodilation.
A circulatory condition where narrowed arteries reduce blood flow to limbs, secondary to atherosclerosis.
Episodic digital ischemia induced by cold, presenting with sequential color changes in digits.
Chronic swelling of tissues caused by an accumulation of lymphatic fluid due to obstruction.
A life-threatening compression of the heart due to rapid accumulation of fluid in the pericardial sac.
The presence of one or more gallstones within the common bile duct, potentially causing biliary obstruction, jaundice, cholangitis, or pancreatitis.
Bacterial infection of the biliary tract caused by obstruction and stasis of bile, classically presenting with Charcot's triad: fever, jaundice, and right upper quadrant pain.
Abnormally dilated veins in the submucosa of the esophagus caused by portal hypertension, typically from cirrhosis; at high risk of catastrophic rupture and massive bleeding.
The transformation of gastric mucosa into a cellular phenotype resembling intestinal mucosa.
Failure of normal intestinal peristalsis leading to functional bowel obstruction; classified as paralytic (adynamic) or mechanical, causing abdominal distension, nausea, and obstipation.
Dyspnea that occurs when lying flat, forcing the individual to remain upright to breathe.

A life-threatening diffuse inflammatory lung injury causing widespread alveolar damage, severe hypoxemia, and bilateral pulmonary infiltrates; most commonly triggered by sepsis, pneumonia, or major trauma.
The fraction of an administered drug dose that reaches the systemic circulation unchanged.
An early, non-specific symptom indicating the onset of a disease before major signs appear.
A pathological condition resulting from a prior, acute disease or injury.

A complex metabolic syndrome associated with underlying illness, characterised by involuntary weight loss, muscle wasting (sarcopenia), fatigue, weakness, and loss of appetite; common in cancer, heart failure, and chronic infection.

A life-threatening subset of sepsis in which circulatory, cellular, and metabolic dysfunction substantially increases mortality; defined clinically by vasopressor requirement and serum lactate >2 mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation.
Abnormally elevated potassium levels in the blood (>5.5 mEq/L), which can cause life-threatening cardiac arrhythmias including ventricular fibrillation.
Surgical creation of an opening in the trachea below the larynx to secure an airway.
A dome-shaped muscular partition separating the thoracic cavity from the abdominal cavity; primary respiratory engine.
One of the two large airways that branch from the trachea into each lung, further subdividing into smaller bronchioles to distribute air throughout lung tissue.
Microscopic air sacs at the terminal ends of the bronchioles where oxygen and carbon dioxide exchange between air and blood takes place across thin capillary walls.
The serous membrane lining the thoracic cavity and investing the lungs.
A flap of cartilage at the root of the tongue, depressed during swallowing to cover the trachea.
The muscular tissue of the heart forming the thick middle layer.
The double-walled sac containing the heart and the roots of the great vessels.
Each of the two upper chambers of the heart that receives blood returning from circulation.
Each of the two large lower chambers of the heart that pumps blood out to organs.
The main artery of the body, supplying oxygenated blood to the systemic circulatory system.
A large vein carrying deoxygenated blood into the heart (divided into SVC and IVC).
The opening from the stomach into the duodenum, surrounded by the pyloric sphincter.
A pouch connected to the junction of the small and large intestines.
A narrow, blind-ended tube attached to the cecum containing dense lymphoid tissue.
The longest part of the large intestine, extending from the cecum to the rectum.
The final straight portion of the large intestine, terminating at the anus.

A large mixed gland located behind the stomach with both exocrine functions (secreting digestive enzymes into the duodenum) and endocrine functions (secreting insulin and glucagon to regulate blood glucose).
The functional unit of the kidney, consisting of a glomerulus and tubule system.
A cluster of small blood capillaries around the end of a kidney tubule filtering plasma.
The muscular tissue duct by which urine passes from the kidney to the bladder.
The duct by which urine is conveyed out of the body from the bladder.
The largest part of the brain, divided into two hemispheres and four lobes, responsible for higher functions including thought, memory, language, sensation, and voluntary movement.
The region of the brain located at the back of the skull responsible for coordinating voluntary movement, balance, and fine motor control.
The central trunk of the brain consisting of medulla, pons, and midbrain continuing to spinal cord.
The three membranes that line the skull and enclose the brain and spinal cord.
The elongated ridges on the floor of lateral ventricles involved in complex memory processing.
The cylindrical bundle of nerve fibers enclosed in the spine connecting body to brain.
A long bone that serves as a strut between the shoulder blade and the sternum.
Technical term for the shoulder blade situated in the superior posterior thorax.
The long flat bone located in the center of the chest connecting to ribs.
The bone of the thigh or upper hind limb, articulating at hip and knee.
Small thick open-ended sesamoid bone protecting the anterior knee joint line.
A layer of fibrocartilage separating adjacent vertebrae acting as a shock absorber.
A short band of tough fibrous tissue connecting two bones or stabilizing joints.
A flexible but inelastic cord of strong fibrous collagen attaching muscle to bone.
The surface epithelium of the skin forming a protective lipid defensive barrier.
The thick layer of living tissue below the epidermis containing capillaries and nerves.
The transparent, dome-shaped outermost layer of the eye covering the iris, pupil, and anterior chamber; responsible for refracting light and protecting the eye from foreign material.

The light-sensitive layer of tissue lining the inner back surface of the eye, containing photoreceptor cells (rods and cones) that convert light into neural signals sent to the brain via the optic nerve.
A fluid-filled spiral structure in the inner ear containing hair cells that convert sound vibrations into electrical nerve impulses transmitted to the brain via the auditory nerve.
Small, bean-shaped organs of the immune system distributed along lymphatic vessels that filter lymph fluid and contain immune cells to detect and fight infection and cancer.
A butterfly-shaped endocrine gland in the neck that produces thyroid hormones (T3 and T4) regulating metabolism, energy, growth, and body temperature.
A hollow, muscular pear-shaped organ in the female pelvis where a fertilised egg implants and a fetus develops during pregnancy; also sheds its lining monthly during menstruation.

A walnut-sized male reproductive gland surrounding the urethra below the bladder that produces seminal fluid nourishing and transporting sperm; commonly affected by benign enlargement and cancer in older men.
Medical specialty combining obstetrics (pregnancy and childbirth) and gynecology (female reproductive system health).
Specialty focused on the diagnosis and management of life-threatening conditions requiring continuous organ support and monitoring.
Surgical specialty focused on diseases of the arterial, venous, and lymphatic systems, excluding the heart and brain.
Surgical specialty dealing with disorders of the colon, rectum, and anus, including cancer, inflammatory bowel disease, and hemorrhoids.
The branch of medicine concerned with the structure and function of the immune system, including the study of allergies, autoimmune diseases, and immunodeficiencies.
Complete loss of consciousness and sensation induced by anesthetic agents, allowing surgical procedures to be performed without pain or awareness.
Reversible loss of sensation in a specific body area by blocking nerve conduction, without affecting consciousness.
Administration of medication to reduce anxiety, induce calm, or produce a sleep-like state, ranging from minimal to deep sedation.
Injection of anesthetic into the epidural space of the spine to block pain in the lower body; commonly used in labour and back surgery.
The funnel-shaped cavity at the centre of each kidney that collects urine from the calyces and channels it into the ureter.
Injection of local anesthetic around a specific nerve or nerve bundle to block sensation in a targeted area without affecting consciousness.
A drug-induced state of reduced consciousness allowing the patient to respond to verbal commands, used for minor procedures.
Injection of local anesthetic into the subarachnoid space of the lumbar spine, producing temporary loss of sensation and movement below the injection site.
Imaging technique using high-frequency sound waves to visualize internal organs and structures in real time; no ionizing radiation involved.
Imaging technique using ionizing radiation to visualize dense structures such as bones and detect pulmonary pathology; the most widely used diagnostic imaging modality.
A substance administered before imaging to enhance the visibility of blood vessels, organs, or lesions on CT, MRI, or X-ray.
Treatment using physical methods such as exercise, manual therapy, and modalities to restore function, reduce pain, and improve mobility after injury or illness.
Therapy focused on helping patients regain the ability to perform daily activities after injury, illness, or disability.
The extent of movement possible at a joint; measured to assess injury, monitor recovery, and guide rehabilitation exercises.
Therapeutic intervention addressing communication, speech, language, voice, and swallowing disorders caused by neurological or developmental conditions.
The use of cytotoxic chemical agents to kill or inhibit the growth of cancer cells, administered orally or intravenously, often causing systemic side effects.
A hollow, muscular organ that pumps blood throughout the body via rhythmic contractions; consists of four chambers — two atria and two ventricles.
Blood vessels that supply oxygenated blood directly to the heart muscle; blockage leads to angina or myocardial infarction.
One of four structures (mitral, tricuspid, aortic, pulmonary) that ensure unidirectional blood flow through the heart chambers.
The smallest blood vessels, forming networks between arterioles and venules where exchange of oxygen, nutrients, and waste occurs between blood and tissues.
A pair of spongy, air-filled organs in the chest that carry out gas exchange — absorbing oxygen and expelling carbon dioxide with each breath.
The hollow space inside the nose that warms, humidifies, and filters inhaled air before it reaches the lower respiratory tract.
Air-filled cavities in the skull bones surrounding the nasal cavity; commonly inflamed in sinusitis.
A muscular, J-shaped organ between the esophagus and small intestine that stores food, secretes gastric acid, and begins protein digestion.
The largest internal organ, located in the upper right abdomen; responsible for metabolism, detoxification, protein synthesis, and bile production.
A long, coiled tube (~6–7 m) between the stomach and large intestine consisting of the duodenum, jejunum, and ileum; the primary site of nutrient absorption.
The final section of the digestive tract (~1.5 m), consisting of the cecum, colon, and rectum; absorbs water and electrolytes and forms stool.
The S-shaped final segment of the colon connecting to the rectum; a common site for diverticulosis and colorectal cancer.
A pair of bean-shaped organs in the retroperitoneum that filter blood, regulate fluid and electrolyte balance, and produce urine.
A hollow, muscular organ in the pelvis that stores urine produced by the kidneys until it is expelled through the urethra.
The central organ of the nervous system enclosed within the skull; controls thought, memory, emotion, sensation, motor skills, and all bodily functions.
A bundle of nerve fibres (axons) that transmit electrical impulses between the central nervous system and the rest of the body.
The fundamental structural and functional unit of the nervous system; specialised cells that receive, process, and transmit information via electrical and chemical signals.
Rigid connective tissue that forms the skeleton; provides structural support, protects organs, enables movement, and produces blood cells in the marrow.
Contractile tissue classified as skeletal (voluntary), cardiac, or smooth (involuntary); responsible for movement, posture, and organ function.
Firm but flexible connective tissue covering joint surfaces, forming the nose and ears, and cushioning between vertebrae; lacks blood vessels and heals slowly.
The long bone of the upper arm extending from the shoulder joint to the elbow; commonly fractured in falls.
The larger of the two lower leg bones; bears most of the body weight and forms the medial side of the ankle joint.
One of the 33 bones forming the vertebral column; protects the spinal cord and provides structural support for the trunk.
A pair of triangular glands sitting atop each kidney; the cortex produces cortisol and aldosterone, the medulla produces adrenaline (epinephrine).
A fist-sized lymphoid organ in the upper left abdomen that filters blood, recycles red blood cells, and plays a role in immune response.
A pair of female reproductive glands that produce eggs (ova) and secrete the hormones estrogen and progesterone.
The lower, narrow portion of the uterus connecting to the vagina; subject to routine screening for cancer via Pap smear.
A pair of male reproductive glands in the scrotum that produce sperm and testosterone.
The coloured, circular muscle of the eye that controls the size of the pupil, regulating the amount of light entering the eye.
A transparent, biconvex structure behind the iris that focuses light onto the retina; becomes opaque in cataract.
A thin, cone-shaped membrane separating the outer ear canal from the middle ear; vibrates in response to sound waves and transmits them to the ossicles.
